resume:
JUNIOR ASSOCIATE — CORPORATE / COMMERCIAL TRANSACTIONS

PROFILE
Licensed attorney (admitted to the State Bar two years ago) with a focus on commercial contracting, vendor and procurement agreements, and general corporate support. I sit at the intersection of the legal team and the business, and most of my day is spent turning ambiguous business requests into clean, signable paper without slowing deals down. I am comfortable owning a contract from intake through signature, escalating only the issues that genuinely warrant partner or general-counsel attention, and keeping a clear audit trail of every position taken. I care a great deal about being the kind of lawyer the business actually wants to call early rather than route around. I read fast, I write tightly, and I try never to send the business a wall of risk without a recommendation attached.

EXPERIENCE

Harbinger Retail Group — Commercial Counsel (Junior Associate), In-House Legal (2023–present)
Harbinger is a mid-market omnichannel retailer with roughly 240 stores and a growing e-commerce arm. I am one of three lawyers supporting a procurement and merchandising organization that signs hundreds of agreements a year, ranging from small marketing-services statements of work to multi-year logistics and SaaS contracts.
- Own the first-pass review and negotiation of inbound commercial contracts: master services agreements, data processing addenda, NDAs, statements of work, and vendor terms. In a typical month I handle thirty-five to forty-five agreements end to end.
- Built and now maintain the company's contract playbook for the procurement team: a fallback-position matrix covering limitation of liability, indemnity, data protection, termination for convenience, and auto-renewal, so that routine deviations can be resolved without escalation. After rollout, the share of contracts that required partner-level escalation dropped from about forty percent to under fifteen percent over two quarters.
- Sit in directly with category buyers during vendor negotiations to give real-time positions on redlines, which cut the average review turnaround on standard agreements from roughly nine business days to four.
- Run the quarterly contract-renewal sweep: I flag auto-renewing agreements ninety days out, summarize spend and risk for the business owner, and recommend renew, renegotiate, or terminate. In the last sweep this surfaced three redundant SaaS subscriptions that the business cancelled, removing a meaningful chunk of recurring spend.
- Partner with the privacy lead on data processing addenda for vendors that touch customer data, mapping each vendor against our data-classification scheme and confirming the right contractual safeguards before signature.
- Maintain the contracts repository and a simple intake form so the legal team has clean metadata — counterparty, value, renewal date, governing law — on every executed agreement.

Vesper & Crowe LLP — Summer Associate, then Contract Attorney (2021–2023)
A regional full-service firm where I rotated through the corporate and litigation groups before concentrating on transactional work.
- Supported partners on M&A due diligence for three lower-middle-market acquisitions, reviewing target contracts for change-of-control, assignment, and exclusivity provisions and drafting issue memos that summarized deal-relevant risk.
- Drafted and revised commercial agreements — supply, distribution, licensing, and confidentiality — under partner supervision, learning the firm's house style and the discipline of tracking every change against client instructions.
- Built a clause bank of negotiated fallback language that the corporate group still uses, organized by provision and by how aggressively each position had historically been accepted.
- Assisted with a contract-remediation project for a client preparing for a financing round, reviewing several hundred customer agreements to identify non-standard terms — uncapped liability, most-favored-nation clauses, unusual termination rights — and logging each into a diligence tracker.

EARLIER EXPERIENCE
Legal Aid Clinic (law school) — Student Advocate
Represented low-income clients in consumer-debt and housing matters under a supervising attorney; drafted answers to collection complaints and negotiated payment arrangements. This is where I learned to translate dense legal exposure into plain language a non-lawyer can act on, and where I got comfortable being the person in the room who has to deliver bad news clearly.

EDUCATION
Juris Doctor — State University School of Law. Activities: Business Law Society; moot court; legal-writing teaching assistant for two semesters.
Bachelor of Arts, Political Science — minor in Economics.

ADMISSIONS & SKILLS
Admitted to the State Bar. Working knowledge of contract lifecycle management tooling, data-protection frameworks, and standard commercial negotiation. I am methodical about version control, comfortable saying 'I need to check that' rather than guessing, and I try to give the business a clear recommendation and a reason, not just a list of risks. I keep a running log of every position I take so that when a partner or the general counsel asks why a term reads the way it does, I can answer in one message rather than reconstructing the history.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
A junior corporate role on a lean in-house team where I can keep owning commercial agreements end to end, take on progressively harder negotiations, and grow toward managing a contract portfolio independently. I want to work somewhere that treats legal as a partner to the business and where being pragmatic about risk is rewarded rather than viewed with suspicion. I would rather be trusted to close routine deals on my own and bring back the two or three issues that actually matter than route everything through a senior lawyer for safety.
jd: Our company is hiring a Junior Corporate Counsel to join a three-person in-house legal team supporting a fast-growing technology business. Reporting to the General Counsel, you will own first-pass review and negotiation of inbound commercial agreements — NDAs, SaaS and vendor contracts, statements of work, and data processing addenda — and help maintain our contract playbook and repository. We want someone who can move quickly without dropping risk on the floor: you will be trusted to resolve routine issues against agreed fallback positions and to escalate only what truly needs the GC. In the interview we will ask you to walk us through a real contract negotiation you handled end to end — the business context, what you were responsible for, the specific positions you took and why, and the concrete outcome. We value clear, structured answers and candidates who can quantify their impact, whether that is turnaround time, escalation rate, or dollars of risk or spend avoided.

history.q: Tell me about your current role at Harbinger and the kinds of agreements you own.
You built a contract playbook for the procurement team — what problem were you solving?
Walk me through one negotiation you ran end to end where you personally held the pen.
What was the single hardest term in that negotiation, and what position did you take?
Did you escalate anything to the GC on that deal, and how did you decide where the line was?
What was the outcome — and can you put any numbers on the risk or time you saved?

candidate_last_answer: Sure. Situation: a logistics vendor sent us their paper for a two-year warehousing contract worth about $1.4M, and their draft had an uncapped indemnity plus a one-sided limitation of liability that excluded their own gross negligence. Task: I owned the negotiation end to end, with a mandate to close within the buyer's three-week deadline and to escalate only if liability stayed uncapped. Action: I redlined the limitation to a 12-month-fees cap with carve-outs for data breach and IP, pushed their gross-negligence exclusion out entirely, and held the indemnity to third-party IP and bodily-injury claims using our playbook fallback. When their counsel pushed back on the cap, I offered a higher 18-month-fees cap rather than reopening the carve-outs, which they took. I escalated exactly one item to the GC — a request for unlimited liability on data breach — and she approved a $3M sub-cap, which I then negotiated in. Result: signed in 16 days, two days inside the deadline; we converted an uncapped exposure into a $3M data-breach sub-cap and a 12-month general cap, and it became a template the other two lawyers now reuse for warehousing deals.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I offered a higher 18-month-fees cap rather than reopening the carve-outs

expected-yield: the candidate's reasoning for why a longer fees cap was a better concession than reopening the data-breach and IP carve-outs — quantifying the relative exposure of each and showing he understood which lever actually protected the company versus which merely looked generous
